blocks.description:
This block is represented by three rounded-edged cards by default with the option to add a fourth. Each card is designed to present content in a visually appealing format, making information easy to digest.
              IMPLEMENTATION
              The block’s layout is structured using two types of tables. One Section Metadata table that defines the overall structure and layout for the collection of cards and multiple Core Card tables that define the content structure for an individual card.
              Section Metadata Table: has two rows and two columns. The first column contains the following properties while the second column contains its value.
              
                Collection-container (mandatory): defines a three-column grid layout by using the value grid-3. You can change this to grid-4 to add a fourth card.
                Collection-item-type (mandatory): identifies that the items in the section will be the core-card type. This value cannot be changed.
              
              Core Card Table: each one represents a single card and has three rows and two columns. The first column contains the following properties while the second column contains its value.
              
                Card_identifier (mandatory): image with recommended resolution of 820x520px. It adjusts to fill the container seamlessly, with cropping applied as needed to maintain the correct aspect ratio and prevent distortion.
                Heading (optional): text styled as Heading 2, with a 60-character limit, including spaces. Text will be cropped if it exceeds this limit.
                Body (optional): text styled as Normal, with a 130-character limit, including spaces. If the text exceeds this limit, it will end with an ellipsis.
                CTA (optional): text linked to a resource styled as Normal, each limited to 30 characters, including spaces. If the text exceeds this limit, it will be cropped. The button's appearance is defined by its formatting.
                  
                    Normal: primary filled button with an oval shape and rounded edges, a solid purple background, and centered white text.
                    Bold: secondary outlined button with an oval shape and rounded edges, featuring a transparent background, a purple border, and centered purple text.
                    Italics: tertiary button without borders, displaying underlined purple text and a chevron, giving it a look that resembles clickable text rather than a traditional button. The chevron needs to be added to the linked text.
                  
                
              
              To implement this block in your site, follow the steps below.
              
                Copy the block and paste it into the document or page.
                Adjust the number of cards by changing the collection-container value if needed.
                Fill in the block's properties according to business needs.
              
              WHEN TO USE
              Cards are a popular UI element in modern web design because they present information in a clear, visually organized manner. The key benefits of cards include:
              
                Content Summarization: Cards are perfect for summarizing individual pieces of content or information, such as blog posts, product details, or user profiles. They allow users to quickly scan through the information and decide what they want to explore further.
                Visual Consistency: When displaying similar types of information, cards create a consistent, structured layout. This consistency helps users to better understand and navigate the content.
                Organizing Related Content: Cards are effective for grouping related information in one place. They are particularly useful when you have a lot of different types of content that you want to present in an organized format.
                Responsive Design: Cards adapt well to responsive design, as they can easily be rearranged to fit various screen sizes. On a large screen, they may appear in multiple columns, while on smaller screens, they stack vertically.
                Highlighting Actions: When you want to highlight key actions or encourage user interaction, cards can feature buttons, links, or other interactive elements in a visually appealing way.
              
              WHEN TO CONSIDER SOMETHING ELSE
              
                If your page contains long-form content or detailed information that requires more space, cards can make the layout feel cluttered and cramped. A more traditional, text-based layout might be better.
                Cards are not ideal for displaying large datasets or complex relationships between data points. In such cases, tables, charts, or graphs may be more suitable.
                If you want users to focus on one particular action or piece of content, cards can introduce too many competing elements. A simpler, more streamlined design might be better for guiding users towards a specific goal.
                When designing for extremely limited space, such as on small mobile screens, cards with multiple elements (image, title, text, CTA) can become cramped. In such cases, a list format or simplified content presentation might be more effective.
                If your content is purely informational and doesn’t require user actions, a card layout may not add any value.
                If the list of cards is extensive and exceeds four items, consider implementing a Cards Carousel for better organization and navigation.

This block is represented by three rounded-edged cards by default with the option to add a fourth. Each card is designed to present content in a visually appealing format, making information easy to digest.
              IMPLEMENTATION
              The block’s layout is structured using two types of tables. One Section Metadata table that defines the overall structure and layout for the collection of cards and multiple Core Card tables that define the content structure for an individual card.

                Collection-item-type (mandatory): identifies that the items in the section will be the core-card type. This value cannot be changed.
              
              Core Card Table: each one represents a single card and has three rows and two columns. The first column contains the following properties while the second column contains its value.
              
                Card_identifier (mandatory): icon referenced as :<iconname>: notation.
                Heading (optional): text styled as Heading 2, with a 60-character limit, including spaces. Text will be cropped if it exceeds this limit.
                Body (optional): text styled as Normal, with a 130-character limit, including spaces. If the text exceeds this limit, it will end with an ellipsis.

                    Normal: primary filled button with an oval shape and rounded edges, a solid purple background, and centered white text.
                    Bold: secondary outlined button with an oval shape and rounded edges, featuring a transparent background, a purple border, and centered purple text.
                    Italics: tertiary button without borders, displaying underlined purple text and a chevron, giving it a look that resembles clickable text rather than a traditional button. The chevron needs to be added to the linked text.
                  
                
              
              To implement this block in your site, follow the steps below.

                Adjust the number of cards by changing the collection-container value if needed.
                Fill in the block's properties according to business needs.

                Highlighting Actions: When you want to highlight key actions or encourage user interaction, cards can feature buttons, links, or other interactive elements in a visually appealing way.

                If your page contains long-form content or detailed information that requires more space, cards can make the layout feel cluttered and cramped. A more traditional, text-based layout might be better.
                If you want users to focus on one particular action or piece of content, cards can introduce too many competing elements. A simpler, more streamlined design might be better for guiding users towards a specific goal.
                If your content is purely informational and doesn’t require user actions, a card layout may not add any value.
                If the list of cards is extensive and exceeds four items, consider implementing a Cards Carousel for better organization and navigation.

blocks.blockName: Branded Default | Image Left | No Offset | White Background
blocks.description:
This block displays content with an image to convey information effectively and enhance user engagement.
              IMPLEMENTATION
              This block's table has two columns. The first column contains the following properties while the second column contains its value.
              
                Image (mandatory): with recommended height of 660px. It adjusts to fill the container seamlessly, with cropping applied as needed to maintain the correct aspect ratio and prevent distortion.
                Eyebrow (optional): Styled as Normal with a 30-character limit, including spaces. Text will be cropped if it exceeds this limit.
                Headline (optional): Formatted as Heading 2, with a 55 and 65-character limit depending on the font used, including spaces. Text will be cropped if it exceeds this limit.
                Content (optional): Styled as Normal text with a 410-character limit, including spaces. If the text exceeds this limit, it will end with an ellipsis.
                Disclaimer (optional): Styled as Normal text with no character limits. It’s recommended to keep the content concise for a clean and effective block presentation.
                Button (optional): text linked to a resource styled as Normal, each limited to 30 characters, including spaces. If the text exceeds this limit, it will be cropped. The button's appearance is defined by its formatting.
                  
                    Normal: primary filled button with an oval shape and rounded edges, a solid purple background, and centered white text.
                    Bold: secondary outlined button with an oval shape and rounded edges, featuring a transparent background, a purple border, and centered purple text.
                    Italics: tertiary button without borders, displaying underlined purple text and a chevron, giving it a look that resembles clickable text rather than a traditional button. The chevron needs to be added to the linked text.
                  
                
              
              WHEN TO USE
              This block is ideal for presenting key visual content that enhances understanding. The key benefits include:
              
                Visual Appeal: Enhances the overall aesthetics of the page, making it more engaging and visually appealing.
                Supporting Content: Reinforces key messages, aiding in communicating benefits, product details, or important concepts.
                Breaking Up Text: Improves readability by providing visual breaks in long sections of text, reducing cognitive load for users.
                Guiding Attention: Directs users’ focus to specific areas or points of interest, like calls-to-action, patient safety information, or visual cues for navigation.
              
              WHEN TO USE SOMETHING ELSE
              
                When visual elements do not add informational value or context.
                If the image is redundant with text or does not align with compliance standards.
                When it can distract from critical text-based information, such as disclaimers or safety details.

example.content:
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Core Image (right)**                                                             |
+------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| image      | ![][image0]                                                           |
+------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| eyebrow    | \[This is an eyebrow]                                                 |
+------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| headline   | ## Required header goes here **lorem ipsum dolor sit amet.**          |
+------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| content    | Required body goes here. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet consectetur       |
|            | adipiscing elit. [Inline link.](https://www.example.com/) More text   |
|            | here. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do |
|            | eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim   |
|            | ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut    |
|            | aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in             |
|            | reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla   |
|            | pariatur. Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in     |
|            | culpa qui officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum.                |
+------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| buttons    | [Primary button link](https://www.example.com/)                       |
|            |                                                                       |
|            | **[Secondary button link](https://www.example.com/)**                 |
+------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| disclaimer | Optional disclaimer goes here. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet consectetur |
|            | adipiscing elit.                                                      |
+------------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
